item.title: Urban Ecosystem Services in Residential Neighbourhoods
item.description:
Research on the benefits associated with nature, or ‘ecosystem services’, has been progressing alongside rising interest in urban design that is both functional and ‘ecologically sensitive’. In Singapore, the Housing Development Board has adopted the ‘Biophilic Town Framework’, which has recently been used to guide the development of various public housing projects. With the adoption of these guidelines, there is a need to monitor the effectiveness of these efforts and compare results across different developments (i.e., post-implementation monitoring of design outcomes).

This project will develop monitoring protocols for environmental quality that can be upscaled to Singapore’s public housing estates and beyond. It will involve the use of remotely-sensed data, as well as the deployment of networked ‘Internet of Things’ (IoT) sensors. The project will focus on five areas of particular relevance to landscape design/monitoring at the neighbourhood-scale:

  1. Heat mitigation and thermal comfort
  2. Noise abatement
  3. Habitat provision for flora and fauna
  4. Soil and water quality
  5. Opportunities for recreation and visual relief

The project is funded from 2021–2024 under the Cities of Tomorrow R&D Programme, administered by the Ministry of National Development and National Research Foundation in Singapore ($1,500,000). It is awarded to the National University of Singapore, in partnership with the Singapore Housing Development Board. The principal investigator is A/Prof. Dr. Tan Puay Yok.


Opportunities

We are looking to hire two Research Assistants in 2021, and offer student projects on topics relating to the biophysical/social components of this project. We welcome enquiries from motivated students, and would love to hear about your specific interest in our work.

item.title: An ecosystem service design loop for using vegetation to mitigate the urban heat island effect
Ecosystem Services in Urban Landscapes
item.description:
Urban greenery can help mitigate the negative impacts of urbanisation, and improve city liveability for urban dwellers. The benefits associated with greenery are realised through the provision of ‘ecosystem services’ such as evaporative cooling, rainfall interception, and green spaces for recreation.

Most research on urban ecosystem services has been conducted in temperate Europe and North America, but we know relatively little about how tropical cities can be designed to enhance ecosystem service provision. Tropical areas, particularly Southeast Asia, are rapidly becoming more urban, with cities growing larger and people living in higher densities. It is therefore important to understand how urban greenery can contribute to enhancing the sustainability and resilience of such cities.

This project uses new types of data and modelling approaches to understand how we can improve the ecological performance of urban greenery. Physical, ecological and socio-cultural parameters are used as inputs to model various types of ecosystem services. Landscape design is performed using 3D point cloud data, to achieve highly accurate representations of existing landscapes. The iterative process of model simulation and design feedback provides opportunities to improve designs based on scientific results.

The project was funded from 2016–2020 by the Singapore National Research Foundation ($2,256,550), awarded to the Future Cities Laboratory, Singapore-ETH Centre. The lead principal investigator was Prof. Peter Edwards.


LiDAR (Light detection & ranging) is used to generate point clouds for landscape design. Project lead: Philipp Urech

Assisting with soil collection at sampling sites across Singapore. Project lead: Tze Kwan Fung

Measuring the interception of rainfall by the tree canopy. Project lead: Rachel Leong

The full team in Singapore
